architectureSummary.overview: WalletVerification is a two-tier application: Angular 16 frontend with OIDC-based login, and an ASP.NET Core 8 API that handles identity verification flows, wallet management (top-up, transfer, withdrawal), and transaction history. SQL Server stores all data with optimistic concurrency on wallet balances.
architectureSummary.authFlow: User clicks 'Login' → Angular redirects to OIDC provider → User authenticates → ID token + access token returned → Angular stores access token in memory → API validates JWT, extracts sub claim → sub claim used as wallet owner identifier

questions.questionText: How did you implement OIDC authentication in WalletVerification?
questions.answer:
WalletVerification uses an external OIDC identity provider (we used a test provider during development, Azure AD in production). Angular handles the OIDC client side using angular-auth-oidc-client library.

Angular OIDC setup:

// app.config.ts
provideAuth({
  config: {
    authority: environment.oidcAuthority,
    redirectUrl: window.location.origin,
    clientId: environment.oidcClientId,
    scope: 'openid profile email wallet:read wallet:write',
    responseType: 'code',  // Authorization Code Flow with PKCE
    silentRenew: true,
    useRefreshToken: true,
  }
})

API JWT validation:

// Program.cs — validate OIDC-issued JWTs
builder.Services.AddAuthentication(JwtBearerDefaults.AuthenticationScheme)
    .AddJwtBearer(options =>
    {
        // Authority = OIDC provider URL — auto-discovers endpoints
        options.Authority = config["Oidc:Authority"];
        options.Audience = config["Oidc:Audience"];
        // The OIDC provider's public key validates the signature
        // — no shared secret needed between API and provider
    });

Resource-based authorization: Each wallet API call checks that the authenticated user's sub claim matches the wallet's owner. This prevents user A from accessing user B's wallet even with a valid token.

var userId = User.FindFirstValue("sub")!;
var wallet = await _walletService.GetAsync(walletId, ct);
if (wallet.OwnerId != userId) return Forbid();  // 403
followUpQuestions: What is PKCE and why is it required for SPAs?
What is the difference between ID token and access token?
questions.importantForInterview: PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) prevents authorization code interception attacks in SPAs. The SPA generates a random code_verifier, sends its hash (code_challenge) with the auth request, then proves it knows the original when exchanging the code for tokens.

questions.questionText: How did you prevent double top-up from concurrent requests in WalletVerification?
questions.answer:
The wallet balance update is a classic concurrency problem — if two requests simultaneously read balance = 500 and add 100, both write 600 instead of 700. I solved this with EF Core's optimistic concurrency:

public class Wallet
{
    public int Id { get; set; }
    public string OwnerId { get; set; } = string.Empty;
    public decimal Balance { get; set; }

    [Timestamp]  // SQL Server rowversion — auto-increments on every update
    public byte[] RowVersion { get; set; } = [];
}

// Top-up operation
public async Task<decimal> TopUpAsync(int walletId, decimal amount, CancellationToken ct)
{
    const int maxRetries = 3;
    for (int attempt = 0; attempt < maxRetries; attempt++)
    {
        var wallet = await _db.Wallets.FindAsync([walletId], ct);
        if (wallet == null) throw new KeyNotFoundException();

        wallet.Balance += amount;

        try
        {
            await _db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
            return wallet.Balance;
        }
        catch (DbUpdateConcurrencyException)
        {
            if (attempt == maxRetries - 1) throw;

            // Reload with current DB values and retry
            await _db.Entry(wallet).ReloadAsync(ct);
            _logger.LogWarning("Concurrency conflict on wallet {Id}, retrying ({Attempt})",
                walletId, attempt + 1);
        }
    }
    throw new InvalidOperationException("Max retries exceeded");
}

EF Core appends WHERE RowVersion = @original to the UPDATE. If the row was modified since we read it, 0 rows are affected and DbUpdateConcurrencyException is thrown. We reload and retry up to 3 times.
followUpQuestions: When would you use pessimistic locking instead?
What is the difference between optimistic and pessimistic concurrency?
questions.importantForInterview: Optimistic concurrency is preferred for low-contention scenarios (most financial UIs). Pessimistic (SELECT ... WITH UPDLOCK) is better when contention is high or you absolutely cannot have a retry loop (e.g., ticket booking).
